response:
The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is the sum of the response times of each database based on the number of queries they handle. Each database's response time depends on its proportion of the total queries. 

For MySQL the response time is $ T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) = a \cdot (\alpha Q)^2 + b \cdot e^{c \cdot \alpha Q} $. For Oracle the response time is $ T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) = d \cdot \ln(\beta Q + 1) + \frac{e}{\beta Q + 1} $. For Sybase the response time is $ T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) = f \cdot ((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q)^{\frac{3}{2}} - g \cdot \sqrt{(1 - \alpha - \beta)Q} $.

The total response time function is the sum of these three functions. This gives $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) = T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) + T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) + T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) $.

To find the optimal values of $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ that minimize $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ we substitute the given constants into the function and evaluate it for $ Q = 1000 $.

The constants are $ a = 0.01 $ $ b = 0.005 $ $ c = 0.02 $ $ d = 0.03 $ $ e = 2 $ $ f = 0.04 $ and $ g = 0.01 $.

The function becomes $ T_{\text{Total}}(1000) = 0.01 \cdot (\alpha \cdot 1000)^2 + 0.005 \cdot e^{0.02 \cdot \alpha \cdot 1000} + 0.03 \cdot \ln(\beta \cdot 1000 + 1) + \frac{2}{\beta \cdot 1000 + 1} + 0.04 \cdot ((1 - \
